content.problemStatement:
Chef loves games! But he likes to invent his own. Now he plays game "Digit Jump". Chef has a sequence of digits S1,S2,…,SNS_{1}, S_{2}, \ldots , S_{N}S1​,S2​,…,SN​. He is staying in the first digit S1S_{1}S1​ and wants to reach the last digit SNS_{N}SN​ in the minimal number of jumps.

While staying in some index iii Chef can jump into i−1i - 1i−1 and i+1i + 1i+1, but he can't jump out from sequence. Or he can jump into any digit with the same value SiS_iSi​.

Help Chef to find the minimal number of jumps he need to reach digit SNS_{N}SN​ from digit S1S_1S1​. 


Input Format
Input contains a single line consist of string SSS of length NNN - the sequence of digits.



Output Format
In a single line print single integer - the minimal number of jumps he needs.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a string S of length N, consisting only of digits (0-9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum number of jumps required to reach the last digit from the first digit.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The minimum number of jumps required to reach the last digit from the first digit is 1, as we can jump directly from the first digit to the last digit.

The minimum number of jumps required to reach the last digit from the first digit is 4, as we need to jump from the first digit to the second digit, then to the fourth digit, then to the fifth digit, and finally to the last digit.
The minimum number of jumps required to reach the last digit from the first digit is 0, as we are already at the same digit.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm. We start from the first digit and explore all possible jumps to adjacent digits or digits with the same value. We keep track of the minimum number of jumps required to reach each digit and update it whenever we find a shorter path.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, where N is the length of the string and M is the total number of jumps.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the visited digits and the queue for BFS.

code.Python3:
import sys
from collections import deque

def solve_one(s: str) -> int:
    n = len(s)
    pos = [[] for _ in range(10)]
    for i,ch in enumerate(s):
        pos[ord(ch)-48].append(i)
    INF = 10**9
    dist = [INF]*n
    visited = [False]*n
    q = deque([0])
    dist[0]=0
    while q:
        u = q.popleft()
        if visited[u]:
            continue
        visited[u]=True
        d = ord(s[u])-48
        for v in pos[d]:
            if dist[v] > dist[u]+1:
                dist[v] = dist[u]+1
                q.append(v)
        pos[d].clear()
        if u-1 >= 0 and dist[u-1] > dist[u]+1:
            dist[u-1] = dist[u]+1
            q.append(u-1)
        if u+1 < n and dist[u+1] > dist[u]+1:
            dist[u+1] = dist[u]+1
            q.append(u+1)
    return dist[-1]

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    out = solve_one(data)
    sys.stdout.write(str(out)+'\n')

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
